original_abstract: Hashing has emerged as a premier technique for efficient similarity retrieval in large-scale multimedia repositories due to its significant savings in storage and rapid query speeds. Recently, supervised multimodal hashing (SMH) has gained traction by incorporating semantic labels to bridge the gap between different data modalities, such as images and text. However, many existing SMH frameworks suffer from high training time complexity—typically quadratic relative to the number of data points—rendering them impractical for truly large-scale applications. In this paper, we introduce a novel approach called Semantic Correlation Maximization (SCM) designed for efficient large-scale data modeling. By seamlessly integrating semantic information without the explicit computation of a massive pairwise similarity matrix, our method achieves a training time complexity that is linear with respect to the dataset size. We propose two learning strategies: one based on orthogonal projections and a sequential learning approach that solves hash functions bit-by-bit via closed-form solutions, eliminating the need for tedious hyper-parameter tuning. Extensive evaluations on the Wiki and NUS-WIDE datasets demonstrate that SCM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art multimodal hashing methods, delivering superior retrieval accuracy while maintaining exceptional scalability.
humanized_abstract: Hashing has attracted increasing attention in the field of efficient similarity search in large-scale multimedia databases, due to its storage-saving property and fast search speed. To further explore the underlying relationship between different modalities and achieve better retrieval performance, many supervised multimodal hashing (SMH) algorithms have been proposed. Unfortunately, most existing works spend a lot of time in the training procedure, especially when the training dataset is large, i.e., their time complexity is generally of order O(n 2 ), which is still unacceptable for large-scale data. In this paper, we propose a new efficient framework called Semantic Correlation Maximization (SCM), which can incorporate semantic information to facilitate the exploration of underlying relationships between data modalities efficiently, and therefore avoid the costly construction of a large similarity matrix. In the proposed SCM, we present two different hashing learning methods: Orthogonal Projection-based Hashing (OPH) and Sequential Hashing (SH). The OPH model employs orthogonal projection to ensure the orthogonal property in the hash code, while the SH model explores each hash code bit sequentially with the closed-form solution to ease the search of optimal hyperparameters. The experiments on Wiki and NUS-WIDE datasets show that SCM outperforms all the state-of-the-art multimodal hashing algorithms with a remarkable speedup, confirming its scalability.
